data.title: Funding Stress Emerges As Liquidity Runs Dry
data.description:
With the reverse repo facility nearly drained and the Treasury refilling its cash pile, liquidity conditions have tightened, driving overnight funding rates higher and reserve balances at the Fed lower.

The TGA has reached approximately $670 billion as of September 11 and is still on pace to reach $850 billion by the end of September. Monday, September 15, is a tax day, and that should accelerate the advance in the TGA, pressuring the reserve lower. Meanwhile, the reverse repo facility has fallen below $30 billion and is no longer a source of liquidity.

Reserves Dropping

As a result of the rising TGA and the reverse repo facility now being drained, the reserve balance has been pushed lower, currently standing at $3.15 trillion. Reserves should continue to decline and are currently on pace to reach around $2.95 trillion by the end of September and potentially lower depending on the type of activity witnessed in the reserve repo facility for quarter-end.

Typically, around month-end and quarter-end, we see an increased usage of the reverse repo facility, as banks look to clear up their balance sheets. The end of September will mark a quarter end, and it is possible that usage could see the reverse repo facility exceed $100 billion.

Rising Borrowing Costs

The effects of falling reserves and the draining of excess liquidity are having an impact on the overnight funding markets, which has resulted in the secured overnight funding rate rising to 4.41%. This increase has also led to the 30-day moving average rate rising to 4.37%. That is actually the highest the average rate has been since the end of January.

This has pushed SOFR 8 bps above the effective funds rate. Overall, this would suggest that liquidity in the market is starting to feel the strain of the Fed balance sheet contraction. While rates have risen, we have not seen activity in the Fed's standing repo facility trigger consistently yet. They would need to start trading around the 4.5% level to really start seeing usage of this facility. That is where the Fed would become a provider of liquidity.

The activity in equity repo financing has fallen over the past few weeks, and this could be directly tied to the increasing overnight borrowing costs. Activity in equity repo financing has a pretty strong relationship with movements and changes in the stock market over time. This is one liquidity source that may continue to struggle if liquidity remains tight.

Fed Cuts Won't Help

Even when the Fed cuts the rate next week, it may not help much because the cost to borrow overnight is likely to remain elevated versus the prevailing Fed Funds rate. The market is already pricing in lower overnight rates following the Fed Funds rate cut, and right now, 2-week swaps are trading around 4.19%, which would be 9 bps higher than the midpoint of the Fed Funds rate range, which would likely be 4.08%. The current overnight swap trades around 4.43%, 10 bps higher than the effective funds rates.

Even once the TGA is filled, the Fed will still have quantitative tightening running in the background, and that will still work to drain reserves. At some point, the Fed will need to stop QT in the not-too-distant future. However, because Treasuries will continue to mature and roll off, it will force the Fed to start buying Treasury bills to keep the balance sheet stable.

This is not to imply that the Fed would be conducting QE, because the balance sheet should not be expanding under this exercise, even though it may first be thought of as such, which could prove to be very messy.

data.title: Trinity Capital: High-Quality BDC, But Waiting For A Pullback (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

The landscape for Business Development Companies is becoming more tricky as interest rates remain elevated. Many BDCs are struggling to grow their earnings and NAV since the higher interest rate environment makes it more costly for businesses to utilize debt financing.

When I previously covered Trinity Capital (NASDAQ:TRIN), I issued a buy rating due to the attractive valuation at the time. Since then, the market has rallied, and TRIN has reported their latest Q2 earnings for the year. Therefore, I wanted to revisit this BDC to provide some updated insights into TRIN's performance and outlook for the remainder of 2025.

Looking at the performance over the last 12 months, we can see that TRIN's share price has increased by roughly 16.9%. TRIN has been able to ride the positive momentum during the market rally over recent months. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 34.5% over the same time frame. TRIN now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 12.6%, which makes it an enticing choice for investors seeking a source of high-income generation. Based on the recent earnings, the distribution is covered by net investment income, but I would like to see the coverage improved in the future.

Following the recent market rally, TRIN now trades at one of the largest premium-to-NAV valuations since inception. Therefore, I no longer believe that it is an attractive time to accumulate shares. TRIN's positive performance over recent quarters may help justify this premium. For instance, non-accruals have remained unchanged, and earnings continue to support the distributions with ease. However, I anticipate that net investment income may temporarily decline when interest rates are cut. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that TRIN implements to generate its earnings.

Portfolio Strategy

According to the latest portfolio breakdown, TRIN has approximately $2.32B in assets at fair value with a weighted effective yield of 15.7% on investments. As a Business Development Company, TRIN generates its earnings by lending capital out to middle-market companies. In exchange, TRIN receives interest income and equity as a way to increase its earnings over time. TRIN's portfolio is unique because it also utilizes some equipment financings and equity investments as a way to generate income. The breakdown by investment type is as follows:

  • Loans: 75.4% - 84 portfolio companies
  • Equipment Financings: 17.3% - 26 portfolio companies
  • Equity & Warrants: 7.3% - 140 portfolio companies

The loan portion of their portfolio is entirely structured as senior secured debt, which sits at the top of the corporate capital structure and has the highest priority for repayment. This acts as a defensive measure for TRIN because it can protect against scenarios where borrowers are struggling to keep up with their required debt maintenance. So if a borrower defaults and needs to liquidate assets, TRIN would be entitled for repayment before other forms of debt.

Expanding on the portfolio diversity, TRIN maintains an efficient allocation of investments across different industries. As we can see below, the majority of the portfolio is allocated to Finance and Insurance investments at 15.6% of the portfolio. This is followed by an allocated to Other Healthcare Services and Medical Devices, which accounts for 11.1% and 10.7% of the portfolio, respectively.

Additionally, 80% of the portfolio is structured as floating rate investments. This helps TRIN capitalize on the higher interest rate environment. Higher interest rates directly translate to a higher level of interest income that can be collected from portfolio companies. However, this also means that TRIN's net investment income levels can decline when interest rates eventually decline.

Updated Valuation

Since TRIN is structured as a BDC, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. As a result, TRIN now trades at a premium to NAV of approximately 21.40%. For reference, TRIN has traded at an average premium to NAV of only 7.05% over the last five-year period. To better conceptualize the current premium level in relation to its historical range, I added the red line to the graph below. As we can see, TRIN now trades at one of the highest premium-to-NAV valuations since its inception.

Wall Street analysts currently have an average price target of $16.44 per share, which indicates a small potential upside of 2.05% from the current level. While this sort of smaller price growth is certainly possible, I do not anticipate any meaningful growth to occur until interest rates are cut. The market seems to be anticipating interest rate cuts to take place this September, related to core CPI data and payroll reports. For reference, interest rates still remain near their all time highs. While this has helped TRIN to collect an elevated level of net investment income, it has also negatively impacted the rate at which TRIN can invest in new opportunities.

For instance, TRIN was able to achieve gross investments funded of $365.5M over the recent quarter. Of this total, $292.3M was allocated to 15 new portfolio companies, while $73.2M was allocated to 14 existing portfolio companies. However, this was offset by sales and repayments of $195M during the quarter, resulting in a net increase of $107.5M during the quarter. So although lower interest rates may negatively impact net investment income in the short term, the favorable borrowing environment may actually cause there to be an influx of companies seeking debt financing. Therefore, TRIN may be able to capitalize on the lower rate environment throughout increased investment activity. So I plan to remain observant and continue to monitor how efficiently TRIN is able to invest in new opportunities going forward.

Q2 Earnings & Risk

TRIN recently reported their Q2 earnings in August, and the results were solid. Most notably, net investment income amounted to $0.53 per share, beating expectations by $0.01. This net investment income level aligns with the prior year's earnings and also represents a slight increase of $0.01 per share from the prior quarter. Total investment income amounted to $69.4M, which is a sizeable year-over-year increase from $53.6M of the prior year. Although total operating expenses have increased to $34.6M, the net investment income still amounted to $34.6M.

TRIN experienced a net realized loss from investments totaling $8.2M during the quarter. However, this is common to see right now across the BDC landscape, since the elevated interest rates make it difficult for portfolio companies to grow their earnings and valuation. Additionally, TRIN's total liabilities now sit at their highest levels ever at $1.12B. This can be attributed to an increase in the KeyBank credit facility of $483M. Despite this, TRIN's leverage ratio still remains reasonable at 1.15x.

One of the most important metrics during the quarter is that TRIN was capable of increasing its NAV. A growing NAV is one of the clearest indications that a BDC's strategy is successful and management is efficiently controlling their capital. Just for reference, here are the quarter-end NAV amounts over the last year:

  • Q2 2024: $13.12 per share
  • Q3 2024: $13.13 per share
  • Q4 2024: $13.35 per share
  • Q1 2025: $13.05 per share
  • Q2 2025: $13.27 per share

When it comes to measuring the risk profile of a BDC, I like to monitor the shifts in the non-accrual rate over time. Non-accruals represent the rate of portfolio companies that are materially underperforming and cannot keep up with their required debt maintenance. As a result, these portfolio companies aren't actively contributing to a BDC's earnings, and they reduce the quality of the portfolio. According to the latest earnings call, non-accruals remain unchanged at 0.9% of fair portfolio value.

Quarter-over-quarter, the number of portfolio companies on nonaccrual improved from 5 to 4. During Q2, 1 new company was added to nonaccrual status, while 2 prior nonaccrual investments were realized and rolled off. As of June 30, nonaccrual credits had a total fair value of approximately $15.6 million or 0.9% of the total debt portfolio, consistent with the preceding quarter. - Gerald Harder, Chief Operating Officer

Another metric that I monitor is the level of PIK (payment-in-kind) interest income that is received. PIK interest income is income that was paid out through the additional issuance of debt or equity, rather than actual cash flow. This is a strategy that allows portfolio companies to defer payments until a later date when cash flows are improved.

Therefore, a growing PIK interest means that there could be some deterioration happening within the portfolio. Thankfully, the latest Form 10-Q indicates that PIK interest income has declined year over year. Therefore, I believe that TRIN is holding up well in this higher interest rate environment and the risks are well managed at this point.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.51 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 12.6%. As previously mentioned, net investment income landed at $0.53 per share for the quarter. This indicates that earnings currently support the distributions at a coverage rate of 104%. While it's great that the earnings support the distribution, I would like to see coverage eventually cross the 110% threshold. A stronger dividend coverage would instill greater confidence in TRIN's ability to keep payouts unchanged over the next 12 months. If interest rates are cut and earnings decline, the distribution could be under threat.

Looking at the dividend history below, we can see that TRIN has managed to keep payouts consistent since its inception. I anticipate that management will continue to prioritize payouts, so I do not expect a cut at this time. During the earnings call, management didn't mention any sort of undistributed spillover income available, which would help alleviate some concerns. So while I don't think there's a reason to sell at this time, I believe investors should just stay observant to the coverage rate over the remainder of 2025.

Additionally, it's worth mentioning that the distributions received from TRIN are fueled by net investment income. Therefore, all income received would be classified as ordinary dividend income, which is taxed at the highest rates for investors. So based on your specific tax bracket, the amount of capital invested, and desired income level, a position in TRIN may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset these potential tax consequences.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am downgrading my rating on TRIN to a hold at this time. The internal structures remain strong, and the overall portfolio quality has improved. For instance, non-accruals are unchanged, but the level of PIK interest income has substantially declined. Additionally, lower interest rate cuts could serve as a positive growth catalyst in the future. However, TRIN trades at one of the highest premium to NAV valuations since its inception, and I would like to see a pullback before accumulating more shares. There is still uncertainty on which direction BDCs can go when the first set of interest rate cuts are implemented.

data.title: Figure Technology: Go Figure
data.description:
Shares of Figure Technology Solutions, Inc. (NASDAQ:FIGR) have made a strong public debut, as the blockchain-enabled financial disintermediator is looking to disintermediate financial services, starting with the issuance of loans which it does on the blockchain.

That looks promising and while the company has seen strong growth, I find it hard to rhyme the promise of disintermediation and reduced costs from a relatively heavy revenue base reported by Figure itself.

This makes me cautious, together with valuation concerns, yet the underlying technology interests me, making me happy to keep a close eye on the business post the offering.

Blockchain Creating Huge Greenfield Opportunities

Figure was founded by Mike Cagney back in 2018, as Mr. Cagney experienced his 'aha' moment, realizing how blockchain might revolutionize financial services with blockchain being a superpower, having the ability to displace trust with trust.

Traditionally, trust in financial services requires intermediation, with multiple parties needed to complete a relatively simple transaction of making a purchase, such as a good or stock, for instance. Blockchain has the power to reduce the number of parties required to just two: the buyer and seller, with all rent-seekers in the intermediate being taken away in such a transition.

However, opportunities are larger as traditional illiquid assets can become liquid, and where transactions take place immediately, eliminating counterparty and settlement risks, but these are all future opportunities of the technology here.

The company focused on providing such offerings by pitching such solutions to banks to originate, aggregate and securitize loans on blockchain. By now, nearly 170 parties use its technology to originate loans on the blockchain. The company started with HELOCs, a home equity line of credit. This was deemed the easiest market to start and securitize, with to date some $16 billion in loans being originated.

Valuation and IPO Thoughts

Figure aimed to sell 31.5 million shares in a preliminary price range between $20 and $22 per share, with final pricing set far above the range at $25 per share. With some 26.6 million shares sold by the company and the remainder offered by selling shareholders, gross proceeds from the offering came in at $931 million, excluding the green shoe option.

With more than 211 million shares outstanding, the company is awarded a $5.3 billion equity valuation at the offer price, which includes about an $800 million net cash position. This definition of cash and debt expanded to loan assets as well.

Total ecosystem volumes for 2024 came in at $5.9 billion, up 75% on the year before. Some $5.1 billion of these volumes related to consumer loan marketplace volumes, complemented by $750 million in digital asset marketplace volumes.

Revenues from these originations came in at $341 million, a number that rose by 58% last year, with the company reporting adjusted EBITDA of $101 million, compared to a modest loss in 2023. Net earnings were reported at $20 million, impressive results for the business.

Somewhat ironic for a business looking to use the blockchain to avoid fees for users of such a system: the company generated revenues equal to nearly 6% of the underlying volumes, quite a steep cut. Note that despite the promise of disintermediation, the company generates revenues from various sources, including loan sale gains, origination gains, interest income, servicing, etc.

Total ecosystem volumes grew by nearly 40% to $3.5 billion in the first half of this year, as revenues grew by just 22% to $190 million, although adjusted revenues came in a bit higher, around $200 million. Adjusted EBITDA of $83 million was rather impressive, with the company generating over $400 million in revenues today and EBITDA seen around $160 million.

With an operating asset valuation of around $4.5 billion at the offering price, sales multiples came in around 11 times, with the company trading around 25 times EBITDA. These appear to be quite realistic multiples given the buzz around the business and the prospects for further growth. Further encouraging news was that second quarter revenues of $106 million revealed some sort of acceleration, reported up 31% on the year before. This followed somewhat of a soft first quarter, as second quarter operating profits of $27 million were rather substantial.

These valuations became a bit more demanding after the first day of trading, as shares ended the day about a quarter higher at $31 per share, after even having traded at highs around $38 per share in the meantime. At these levels, operating assets are valued closer to $6 billion, pushing up sales multiples to about 15 times, although that operating earnings multiple annualise at 50-60 times.

Concluding Thoughts

Frankly, there are quite some risks here. While strong growth, ample growth opportunities and profitability being achieved are real achievements, there are risks as well.

This includes a limited operating history, reliance on key staff, but also other structural risks like rapidly evolving competition, a reliance on the HELOC market, partner concentration issues, evolving technologies, and changes in the regulatory framework.

For me, the big question remains how exactly the business model works. After claiming that blockchain can cause disintermediation, Figure itself plays some role as an intermediary (or at least as a supplier to the industry) with these fees looking relatively high in relation to the processed volume base.

data.title: Esquire Financial Holdings: Shares Are Finally Ready For A Downgrade
data.description:
One financial firm that I have had a very good track record with over the last couple of years now has been Esquire Financial Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:ESQ). For example, since reaffirming the company as a "Buy" candidate in April of this year, the stock has seen upside of 37.6%. That's well above the 20.4% increase that the S&P 500 has seen over the period. But that's nothing. Since I originally rated it a "Strong Buy" in June 2024, the stock has surged by 114.1%. By comparison, the broader market is up only 19.8% since then. Fundamentally speaking, Esquire Financial Holdings has demonstrated itself to be a high-quality business. Asset readings are off the chart, and overall growth for the company has been positive. However, high-quality names like this don't come cheap. In fact, in many respects, this is a rather expensive prospect. And it has gotten so pricey, in fact, that I believe it's finally time to downgrade it once again, this time to a "Hold" rating.

Time to take a step back

If you have never come across Esquire Financial Holdings before, that's understandable. With a market capitalization of $771.5 million as of this writing, it's not exactly a household name. And even though the company is a financial institution, it really does serve a niche purpose. I say this because, as opposed to a traditional bank, it focuses on catering to the litigation industry. It provides customers in this space with loans dedicated to meeting working capital requirements, dispersing funds from cases settled or won, and more. However, this niche focus affords the company rather impressive financial results.

Perhaps the best place to start would be to discuss the trend when it comes to deposits. At the end of the most recent quarter, the company had $1.78 billion worth of deposits. That's up nicely from the $1.64 billion in deposits the institution had at the end of last year and the $1.41 billion that it had the year before that. Usually, whenever I look at deposits, I worry about uninsured deposit exposure. I typically like this to be no greater than 30% of total deposits. As of the end of the most recent quarter, 31.5% of its deposits were uninsured. Although this is above the maximum I prefer, it's not terribly above. Furthermore, around 75% of its uninsured deposits are with clients that have what management considers to be a full commercial relationship with it. Management defines this as customers that use it not only for deposit services, but also for commercial loans, payment processing services, or other things.

As the value of deposits has increased, the value of loans has also increased. At the end of last year, they came in at $1.38 billion. But today, they stand at $1.48 billion. The largest chunk of this money involves commercial loans. That works out to roughly 67.5% of the total. And in fact, in recent years, this side of the loan portfolio has been growing at a disproportionately fast rate. At the same time, other categories like multifamily loans, commercial real estate loans, and one-to-four-family loans have essentially remained flat or increased only slightly. The benefit to these loans, according to management, is that they are variable rate and offer a rather significant yield. They also seem to be pretty high quality, as evidenced by the fact that almost all of those loans are tied to prime arrangements. And even though variable rate loans create risk for the company if interest rates decline, as they certainly will soon, 90% of its variable rate commercial loan portfolio has interest rate floors in place.

The value of securities in the most recent quarter came in at $325 million. That represented a small increase over the $313.4 million that the company had at the end of last year. Cash and cash equivalents, meanwhile, popped from $126.3 million to $163 million. Even though prices across the board like this are often associated with a rise in debt as well, what makes Esquire Financial Holdings special is that management continues to have no debt on the firm's books. This makes the risk profile for shareholders quite low and makes the uninsured deposit exposure much more tolerable.

When it comes to the income statement, we have seen pretty consistent growth as of late. For the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, as an example, net interest income totaled $25.7 million. That was up from the $23.3 million the company reported the previous year. This was even though the net interest margin for the company contracted from 6.19% to 6.03%, and its provision for credit losses fell from $1 million to $3.5 million. It can be attributed to the continued growth of the business. And for the first half of this year, the $51.8 million in net interest income generated by the company beat out the $45.2 million reported the year before that. And once again, this was even though the provision for credit losses for the company grew and the fact that its net interest margin dropped.

Non-interest income has also increased recently. For the second quarter of 2025, it came in at $6.6 million. That's up from the $6.3 million reported the previous year and was despite the fact that payment processing fees dropped from $5.3 million to $5.1 million. According to management, this decline was because of the firm's decision to change its merchant risk profile and composition. Management continues to focus here on expanding sales channels using ISOs (Independent Sales Organizations), though it has, by its own admission, focused more on ensuring that its risk profile is lower even as the number of small business merchants that it supports grew from 88,000 at the end of last year to 92,000 today. The growth in non-interest income, then, was primarily because of a $0.4 million gain on one of its equity investments that the company sold during the quarter. But that wasn't enough, unfortunately, to prevent this metric from remaining flat for the first half of this year at $12.7 million compared to the same time last year.

The overall increase in revenue for the company has made it possible to increase overall profitability, too. For the second quarter of 2025, net income came in at $11.9 million. That represents an increase of 13.3% compared to the $10.5 million that the company reported the previous year. Thanks to this strong showing, net income for the first half of 2025 amounted to $23.3 million. That happens to be 13.7% above the $20.5 million that the company reported for the first half of the 2024 fiscal year.

Unfortunately, a company of this nature does not come cheaply. If we project out results for the rest of this year based on what the company has seen for the first half of the year, the firm would still be trading at a price-to-earnings multiple of 15.5. That is materially greater than the 10 trading multiple that I typically like to see as a maximum. As the chart above illustrates, it also puts the company well above any of the five companies that I compared it to. In the chart below, I also compared it to the same five firms on both a price-to-book and price-to-tangible book basis. I understand that such a high-quality, niche business deserves to trade at a nice premium compared to other banks. But the disparity here is massive.

Some investors might argue that overall asset quality helps bridge this gap. For quite some time, I certainly felt that way. As an example, in the first chart below, you can see Esquire Financial Holdings’ return on assets. With a reading of 2.37%, the company is far more profitable than most other banks that I look at. For context, I typically consider a return on assets reading of 1% to be attractive. As that chart illustrates, our candidate is massively higher than any of the five competitors I stacked it up against. In the chart below that, I did the same type of comparative analysis using return on equity. The 18.74% rating that we get here is materially greater than the 10% minimum that I like to see. And it also puts the company quite a bit above any of the other five firms.

Takeaway

All things considered, I would say that Esquire Financial Holdings is doing quite well. Fundamentally speaking, the company continues to thrive. However, this does not mean that it makes for an attractive prospect right now. After seeing such monumental upside, I would argue that further market-beating potential is probably limited. Given this, I have no choice but to downgrade ESQ to a "Hold" rating for now.

data.title: Broadcom: Exposure To ASIC Is Another Possibility In The AI Race
data.description:
Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) is the larger player in solution specific chipsets that are a crucial part of the AI revolution, coexisting with GPUs. The company also develops and produces analog products that are high in demand and face strong competition. After its results release, I am updating company financials and reassessing its suitability to equity portfolios.

Investment Thesis

Broadcom is a company that operates in two different markets within the semiconductor arena. If one tries to map the chip final production, it may find some important distinctions between memory suppliers, both DRAM and NAND, which are dominated by Korean players, CPU suppliers, both X86 and RISC, generally used for PCs and Smartphones, the AI driven chips, which is the first Broadcom game field, and the analog chips, the other market where the firm acts, used for computing, ethernet connections, sensors, the BA2 Plus I used in my CFA exams, and in almost infinite number of applications.

Broadcom does not disclose the percentage of its business being generated by the two businesses. It rather divides segments as hardware and software. On its report, on the other hand, it is possible to locate the AI activity as a result of the sales of customized silicon solutions to data centers and service providers as part of its Networking offering. And in here, the word customized is very relevant and Broadcom provides ASIC (Application Specific Integrated Circuits) chipsets to AI hyperscalers and other players. This kind of chips are designed to run specific applications and software with maximum efficiency, as opposed to CPUs, or more relevantly GPUs, that are designed to perform many different tasks in distinct software landscapes and keep a sort of multi-task efficiency. Broadcom designs products that may be a combination of RISC (investors may look at Arm Holdings – ARM), PowerPC and proprietary solutions aiming at achieving a high-performance blend to these top-tier chipsets.

Main advantages of ASIC operation are related to efficiency, including power consumption and heat dispersion, and a general reduced life-cycle cost, especially when it is produced in certain scale. As it is understandable, the usage of ASIC semiconductors limit flexibility, what can be potentially damaging when underlying applications are not totally mature or evolve rapidly and disruptively. Also easy to understand, the main competitor is Nvidia and its powerful GPUs. In previously written articles, it was evident how CUDA commanded an environment for the usage of its GPUs, which include competitive advantages in the GPU market but also in regard to the selection of GPUs in detriment to ASICs.

If nowadays, GPUs dominate at a 3:1 ratio, future trends point to ASIC gaining ground as inference for AI reasoning become a central piece in systems. In addition, most analysts view Broadcom as one of the leaders in ASIC market. Future scenario delineates the coexistence of GPUs and ASICs, maybe not with a different participation ratio in terms of market share but potentially but volume gain in ASICs. The takeaway here is that all the tailwinds described to Nvidia in AI are also applicable, then, to Broadcom in this business line.

In the case of analog chips, Broadcom supply chips to mobile connectivity, storage systems, broadband connection, and industrial applications, among others, in a very competitive, low entrance-barrier market disputed by companies like Texas Instruments (TXN), Analog Devices (ADI) and On Semiconductor (ON). This segment is very diverse, but it is not subjected to the same AI push described to ASIC. Moreover, the fierce competition drives down margins in this segment, compared to specialized solutions to AI inference, where price power is relevant. It is not a bad market to be in tough. Expected annual growth is viewed as around 7% focused on digitalization, power efficiency, EVs and Smart Cities. Other sources temper this growth to lower levels due to continuous price pressure.

Broadcom, in this sense, is a blend that can offer the exposure to strong AI expected growth with a steady, more mature market that can release decent value to fund expansion and reward investors. On the 4th of September, the company released another report, so let’s break it down to understand how those trends are playing firm’s financials.

Personal Note: despite reporting on September 4th, it was only one week ago that the 10-Q became available, including the data on the SEC system, EDGAR. From an analyst perspective, a bit disappointing.

Broadcom Financial Update

In my research, I compare several target firms that are selected based on an appropriate investment thesis. Right now, some of the relevant Theses include Artificial Intelligence Hardware and Applications, Infrastructure and Cybersecurity, among others. Some comparisons in this article will be made against this broad group and some will be made with the direct competitor in the ASIC market, Marvell Technologies (MRVL). An important point to consider is that Broadcom constituted an income tax provision as a result of a transfer of intellectual property to the US as part of a supply chain rearrangement. This action may result in the need of adjustments in the yearly comparison. Firms are compared in terms of four elements. The first is related to the Cash Discipline trying to evaluate how well the operation converts cash. The second is related to the total yield an investor receives considering the pay-out and the potential reinvestment of cash at current prices. The third category is growth, to understand commercial success and how it translates into real profitability growth. The last one is a measure of momentum, as it is a strong performance indicator and also may impose important demand engine due to the amount of passive investments.

In the quarter, Broadcom continued to generate sales growth and margin expansion. Revenues achieved US$ 15.9 Billion, the largest quarter in history. This growth combined with strong margin performance is somewhat similar to the observed in Nvidia and Marvell, indicating strong demand resilience for AI chipsets, and reinforce that both GPU and ASIC solutions may coexist in the market. When compared directly in the ASIC market, Broadcom sales growth seems more consistent in terms of being a longer trend compared to Marvell, despite the competitor’s effort to compensate in the last year. Figures 1 and 2 illustrate the top line performance of Broadcom and the revenue comparison with Marvell.

Figure 3 depicts the historical margin performance of Broadcom. The analysis of the chart reveals that the company has engaged in aggressive acquisitions to boost its growth. In comparison with Nvidia, it unveils a lower quality growth engine, that must be taken into consideration. In February of 2024, the firm included the numbers from the acquisition of VMware in its books, generating an immediate increase in operating leverage that was only partially recovered in time, as synergies failed to be executed. Before the acquisition, the sum of SG&A, R&D and Intangibles Amortization represented around 28% of sales. In February, this ratio increased to 51%, and in the last quarter it is still at a 39% level. Based on these numbers, the changed profile in operating leverage impacted the bottom line in a permanent way. Moreover, as explained, in the quarter ended in August 2024, the additional provision of income tax generated a negative net income, which will not impact cash generation as this was a non-cash provision.

VMware was acquired at its market cap, plus US$ 8 Billion in debt, generating a small premium. Acquisition process took 17 months to conclude. Nevertheless, the goodwill increased US$ 53 Billion in the quarter. VMware was a leader in virtualization, that occurs when a certain local application from a customer is moved to a cloud and offered as a software subscription from that moment on. Broadcom, wanted to increase presence in the software business. Since the acquisition, the company succeeded in increasing revenues, but mainly due to customer migration to more expensive plans, at the expense of a reduced number of customers in the business. This trend is a double sword situation, mostly as new competitors, like Nutanix (NTNX) try to expand market share.

Personal Note: When I worked in Telecom, I remember VMware becoming a player in core network virtualization, resulting in virtual packet core and OSS/BSS. This resulted in deals with telecom operators, disrupting a market that was captive to incumbent telecom suppliers, based on robust business case in favor of network virtualization. They had compelling offers, mostly for smaller and geographically dispersed networks.

Despite the impacted performance from VMware acquisition, Broadcom margin is still much better than its direct ASIC competitor. The main hypothesis for this trend is related to scale achieved by Broadcom allows for healthier margins, highlighting the need to increase top line by Marvell. Figure 4 indicates the margin difference on its gross and net income measures. The acquisition also impacted balance sheet accounts. Figure 5 depicts the reduction of liquidity measures as a result of invoicing and revenue recognition on the software business, which can be considered a new pattern for Broadcom. After acquisition, current ratio stands in its limit around 1 until this report that depicted a relevant increase in liquidity. Again, we have seen a deterioration on financials since the acquisition, but also a potential recovery trend that would boost Broadcom’s valuation.

Similarly, the company observed an increase in the goodwill and intangibles. This is the result of the acquisition accounting routine, in which the excess paid value in comparison with the assets of the target firm is accounted as goodwill, or intangibles when an identifiable non-physical asset is involved in the transaction, such as a brand. In the case of Broadcom and VMware transaction, most of the intangibles were identified as “developed technology”. It may underline a potential overpayment in the transaction.

On Figure 6, it is possible to evaluate the operating cash flow performance of Broadcom. The firm has consistent cash generation, in line with its profitability as a result of most of the result being attributed to sales operations. Moreover, the firm has a positive track record in terms of operating efficiency related to DSO (Days of Sales Outstanding) and ITO (Inventory Turnover). Even in such situation, the company had to count with debt issuance to be able to pay for the acquisitions and remunerate investors through dividends and buy-backs, as illustrated by Figure 7. In this chart, the rolling last four years of cash measures are scaled by Net Sales, as per comparison possibilities. Broadcom has an active US$ 10 Billion buyback program and pays regular dividends, considered safe.

As a result, the company has positive financials with very strong prospects but, in the comparison with the other target firms analyzed, Broadcom presents a challenging result, due to the elevated debt level and the mild free cash performance. Broadcom is the red dot on Figure 8. For the future, investors may understand performance between one of the two extreme scenarios. The up-case is the one in which the company is able to fully absorb all synergies expected from VMware acquisition, and the company is able to produce organic growth and cash expansion from the robust tailwind in the AI business. The down-case is the one where, even with unproven synergies from the acquisition, the firm engages in additional potentially expansive acquisitions focusing on top-line, depressing its ability to fully absorb the positive market trend and keep up with investor pay-out and reducing Return on Investment on future cash usage. Up to now, it a conclusion seems still unclear, imposing risks on future cash forecast. Right now, the firm receives a C+ grade on the forward PEG ratio.

Conclusion

The combined evaluation of the four elements in the research position Broadcom as a Buy. It means investor may evaluate its suitability to integrate equity portfolios, diversifying within AI. The company is able to generate consistent cash results and has a strong tailwind from the AI demand, that may benefit ASIC processors. At this point, Broadcom dominates ASIC and may be able to enjoy from this market movement. The company, on the other hand, has an important homework on fully capturing synergies form acquisitions, in order to boost investor returns. When compared to Nvidia, it seems a riskier bet, with the benefit of product diversification within the AI thesis. Investors seeking exposure on ASIC, or complimentary exposures to existing portfolio positions towards AI may evaluate Broadcom carefully in this regard.

data.title: Coupang: The Real Growth Potential Could Be In Front
data.description:
E-commerce platform Coupang Inc. (NYSE:CPNG) has delivered a strong performance in 2025, but the real growth may be further down the line.

An introduction to Coupang

Coupang Inc. is an e-commerce platform founded in South Korea in 2021 but now headquartered in Seattle, Washington. The company is often labeled the 'Amazon of South Korea' and has expanded into food delivery, fintech, and video streaming. The company operates more than 100 global fulfillment centers with a large fulfillment and logistics workforce, which is increasing in automation.

Coupang showing strength in weakened markets

Coupang Inc. has seen its share price surge by around 45% year-to-date, and a recent price boost came from its second quarter earnings release on August 5. Takeaways from the earnings release were a 16% year-over-year revenue increase to $8.5 billion and stronger margins, with a nearly 230 basis points to 32.6%.

The company is developing a strong footprint in Taiwan with quarterly revenue growth of 23% in Q4, surging to a 54% quarterly gain in the current quarter. Year-over-year revenue growth reached triple digits in Q2, with management expecting a further improvement in Q3.

The initial point I would make on the Buy recommendation is that Coupang has been operating in Asian markets with an economic headwind. China's recovery from the lockdown era has been slower than expected and can continue to pick up steam in the months and years ahead.

"Our investments in customer experience continue to drive deeper customer adoption and engagement, fueling the durable revenue growth at high multiples of the relatively flat Korean retail market," CEO and founder Bom Kim said in the recent earnings call.

Seeking Alpha noted that tech-focused hedge fund Whale Rock Asset Management bought 1.41M shares in Q2.

Automation, AI, and cloud driving growth

Coupang has embraced an operating system that is heavily reliant on technology with an AI-driven management system that runs from customer order to delivery. The company employs robotics for stacking and moving products around its fulfillment centers.

Bom Kim added in the latest earnings call that AI is already core to operations and will be a "long-term enabler of both top line growth and margin expansion."

Coupang is also at the early stages of potential growth in its cloud offering. The company has used cloud to enhance its e-commerce platform, but recent government contracts have analysts looking at expansion. The current GPU-as-a-Service (GPUaaS) business segment could expand as AI use cases grow.

One area that Coupang specializes in is bringing overseas brands to its markets, rather than the opposite. A recent success was California's POM entering the Korean market via the platform. POM Arils was awarded 'Best New Product' in the Coupang marketplace and appeals to health-conscious Asian consumers. Coupang can leverage that opportunity as it expands its presence in markets such as Taiwan.

A deeper look at earnings and valuation

The response to the Q2 earnings was positive on revenue growth but weighed down by a drop in free cash flow. However, we can see from the statements that the decline was fully driven by investments in property, land, and equipment.

I believe that investors focusing on the short-term noise could miss out when Asian economies strengthen.

Adjusted earnings before interest and taxes were $431M higher year-over-year, with a 67-basis point margin improvement to 4.9%. Because I see this as a growth opportunity, it is better to look further out on revenue. We can see that revenue has more than doubled since 2020, and that is in a tougher economic environment for Asia. The company's cost of revenue has matched the sales growth, and that is hampering income, alongside the growth investments. However, this should improve with economic growth, and the company has potential to expand on its cloud operations.

The current financial dynamics are showing up in the valuation ratios with a very high price/earnings ratio of 196.83x on a GAAP forward basis. However, the price/sales ratio of 1.68x is much more appealing, despite a premium of 73% over the sector.

For comparison, Amazon (AMZN) has a P/S ratio of 3.47x, showing that there is strong upside potential for Coupang stock with a revival of Asian economic growth and a maturing profitability within the company.

Management has also noted scaling inefficiencies in Taiwan, which is common with early-stage market entries and should become more favorable with time.

Risks to the investment thesis

Coupang operates in a tough environment with more and more companies embracing e-commerce since the pandemic. Overseas expansion opportunities may be limited, and that means the company will have to bet on an economic revival in China and the broader region. Despite this, I believe the company is showing organic growth in flat markets, while they are also at the forefront of AI systems and efficiencies. The price/earnings ratios are high, but the price/sales shows that the company has some value in the current price.

Conclusion

Coupang began life as an 'Amazon Killer' but is paving a path for itself that can stay out of the crosshairs of the Amazon juggernaut. The company's specialization in bringing overseas brands into Korea and Taiwan should bode well in the future, especially if the Chinese economy strengthens. That could also happen at the expense of a slowdown in Western economies, and I believe that it makes a more compelling opportunity than Amazon's maturing model. Coupang has growth opportunities forming in areas like streaming and the cloud. The short-term finances should be viewed in that manner, and the recent cash flow dip was simply an investment into tangible assets.

data.title: Glaukos' Pipeline And High Valuation: A Risky Hold With Long-Term Upside
data.description:
Glaukos Corporation (NYSE:GKOS) stock price has been having a bad year with double-digit losses. However, over the past month, the stock has been showing some recovery signs, probably backed by an improved Q2 outturn. The company experienced an improvement in almost all its metrics, including revenue and gross margin, and displayed strong pipeline depth. That said, GKOS stock was flagged by Seeking Alpha last month as a stock at high risk of performing badly because it is 'overpriced and has negative EPS revisions when compared to other Health Care stocks'. The stock attracted a Sell rating on the Quant rating system, meaning it had massively underperformed the S&P 500. This rating still came after the release of Q2 results, which I feel were impressive. Despite the harsh Quant rating system flag, I'm of the opinion that GKOS stock might deserve some patience given its potential. I would rather HOLD Glaukos stock for now and watch its transition, as I believe it might in the near term.

Glaukos Corporation is a global ophthalmic medical technology and pharmaceutical company dedicated to innovative therapies for glaucoma, corneal disorders, and retinal diseases. The company pioneered Micro-Invasive Glaucoma Surgery (MIGS), launching its first device in 2012, and has since expanded into drug-delivery implants and corneal/retinal treatments. Headquartered in Aliso Viejo, California, with global operations, Glaukos completed its IPO in 2015, advancing eye care through novel, lasting treatment platforms.

How Glaukos Generates Revenue

Glaukos, being a medical tech company, focuses on eye diseases, namely glaucoma, corneal disorders, and retinal diseases.

Glaucoma is a disease that damages the optic nerve, often from high eye pressure. Corneal disorders are those problems affecting the clear front surface of the eye. Meanwhile, retinal diseases like macular degeneration affect the back of the eye.

For glaucoma treatments, Glaukos' flagship products are micro-invasive glaucoma surgery (MIGS) devices like the iStent. These are tiny stents doctors implant during cataract surgery to help fluid drain from the eye. This lowers eye pressure, thereby slowing glaucoma damage. This revenue line is Glaukos' current core revenue driver.

Meanwhile, the company sells systems like iLink for corneal cross-linking. The systems are used in treating keratoconus, a disease that weakens and thins the cornea. This procedure strengthens the cornea using special drops and UV light, preventing worsening vision.

For retinal diseases, such projects are still in the pipeline and yet to be commercialise.

Brief Q2 Financial Highlights

Glaukos delivered a strong Q2 2025 performance, with record net sales of $124.1 million, representing a 30% increase year-over-year (YoY). This growth was lifted by its glaucoma segment, which saw net sales of $103.5 million, up 36% YoY.

Meanwhile, U.S. glaucoma sales were particularly robust, surging 45% to $72.3 million from the previous year. The company's non-GAAP gross margin improved to 83%, and as a result, the company raised its FY2025 sales guidance to between $480 million and $486 million.

I believe these results highlight solid operational execution and the successful launch of key products like iDose TR.

What's in Glaukos' Pipeline?

Glaukos is seeing beyond its current operations, with Epioxa being the most immediate in the pipeline. Epioxa is a non-invasive corneal cross-linking therapy for keratoconus treatment. The drug's new drug application (NDA) was accepted by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), while its Prescription Drug User Fee Act (PDUFA) decision is due October 20, 2025. If approved, the drug could launch in 2026, replacing Photrexa while creating a better corneal revenue stream.

Meanwhile, the glaucoma segment remains anchored by iDose TR, a drug that is already commercial and delivering sustained drug release. The iDose Reimplantation NDA is under review, with a decision expected in January 2026. The good thing is that after the review, it'll create a recurring refill cycle from that year forward. That said, iDose TREX, with greater drug capacity, is now in Phase 2b/3 trials, with approval possible around 2027–2028.

Beyond iDose, Glaukos is pushing the PreserFlo MicroShunt, which is already approved in Europe, Canada, and Australia. The drug is in pivotal U.S. trials and is getting well-positioned for a potential FDA submission in the next few years and a possible U.S. approval around 2027.

For the longer term, the company's ILution platform and GLO-401 retinal therapy, currently in Phase 2, represent opportunities likely beyond 2028.

Key Risks to Watch

Product Transition: From Photrexa to Epioxa

Glaukos is expecting a PDUFA approval next month, October 2025, for Epioxa, which, if approved, will result in a commercial launch in 2026, and the drug could replace Photrexa.

Management has warned of a possible transient disruption as patients and providers might delay Photrexa procedures in anticipation of Epioxa. The Q2 earnings call has flagged a likely Q4 revenue impact as the market shifts. The bad part of this development is that Photrexa revenue was already under pressure after entering the Medicaid Drug Rebate Program (MDRP), which reduced realized Photrexa revenue.

The major downside in my view is that if payer coverage is slow post-approval or if clinicians wait for post-launch experience, 2026 corneal revenue could be lower than modeled and will produce a quarter(s)-long revenue dip.

On the other hand, the glaucoma side with its iDose TR is already contributing strongly, yet its long-term revenue durability depends on the FDA’s decision on iDose Reimplantation, with a PDUFA date of January 28, 2026. I think a positive outcome would create a recurring revenue cycle from 2026 onward, while a denial would limit iDose to a one-time implant.

In parallel, iDose TREX is in Phase 2b/3 trials, and PreserFlo is progressing in U.S. studies, offering mid-decade upside. Together, these transitions represent near-term volatility but significant long-term growth opportunities. I think the bad side of things is how Glaukos' products are heavily dependent on approvals.

Stronger Competition

Glaukos is not alone in its space, and it looks like its competitors are bigger and have been performing and executing much better. Players like Regeneron and Roche are strong in the ophthalmology sector, particularly retina.

Regeneron is already generating nearly $6 billion annually through its blockbuster anti-Vascular Endothelial Growth Factor (VEGF), Eylea. This competitor carries the financial muscle to dominate research and development (R&D), marketing, and payer access.

On the other hand, Roche is a leader in innovation. The company's notable product is Vabysmo, which extends dosing to 16 weeks, coupled with its refillable Susvimo implant and broad retinal pipeline.

Sadly, Glaukos's retinal efforts have remained early-stage with its products, GLO-401 and iLution. To fight this kind of competition, Glaukos must apply precision in its operations by scaling surgeon training, winning reimbursement, and innovating in niches.

Overall, the space is not looking so friendly for Glaukos, and the company ought to work so hard.

Reimbursement Risk

It's important to note that doctors will only use Glaukos' devices and drugs on the condition that insurers reliably pay them back. Considering that most glaucoma patients are older, Medicare and Medicaid coverage in the U.S. is critical.

In glaucoma, coverage varies as some Medicare Administrative Contractors (MACs) reimburse iDoseTR, while others lag, slowing adoption. Nationally, the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) has cut fees for older MIGS (Micro-Invasive Glaucoma Surgery) stents, pressuring surgeon economics and nudging shifts to newer options.

In corneal health, Photrexa revenues are reduced by Medicaid rebates, while Epioxa (FDA decision October 2025) still needs billing codes and payer approval, potentially delaying uptake.

Overall, I think that Glaukos’s reimbursement risk creates short-term bumps due to uneven coverage, and fee cuts slow adoption. Should Medicare and insurers gradually align, I foresee long-term visibility improving, thereby supporting stable, recurring revenues from new therapies.

Glaukos Cash Management

From the latest Q2 results, Glaukos appears financially capable of taking care of its ambitious pipeline without necessarily rushing back to raise cash.

I say so because it managed to deliver over $4 million in underlying operating cash during the quarter, showing it can almost cover itself despite heavy investment. The company's headline $25 million cash decline was tied to a one-off new headquarters (HQ) and the Mobius (MitoSol) acquisition, and that expense was not linked to day-to-day operations.

I also believe that with 83% gross margins, Glaukos generates a lot of cash from each sale, which helps offset its FY25 expected operating expense figure of around $460 million. Meanwhile, the company's debt is almost negligible at around 2% of capital, while liquidity is very strong with a 6.5x current ratio. This means that Glaukos can comfortably pay its near-term obligations.

This is despite the fact that the company posted losses in Q2 (EPS -0.24) and that it has accumulated deficits from years of R&D. Such a trend is not out of bounds but a common phenomenon, especially for medtech innovators.

I still believe that Glaukos, with disciplined spending, low debt, and strong gross margins, can fund its near- and mid-term projects (Epioxa, iDose Reimplantation, PreserFlo) from internal resources while retaining flexibility for later-stage retinal bets.

High Risk of Performing Badly

As I highlighted earlier, Glaukos' stock is looking overvalued on Seeking Alpha quant screens, a perception I partly share as well. That said, I would also think that the stock might deserve the benefit of the doubt, given its pipeline and market position.

Considering that iDoseTR is ramping quickly and should Epioxa’s FDA decision in October 2025 be positive, it could open a new corneal revenue stream in 2026. With 83% gross margins, minimal debt, and near cash-flow breakeven, Glaukos can self-fund innovation.

Additionally, considering that ophthalmology markets like glaucoma and keratoconus are chronic and growing, they could offer lasting growth momentum. My other sense thinks that if execution stays on track, today’s premium multiples could normalize as revenues inflect in 2026–2028.

Valuation

Glaukos' forward Price/Sales ratio of 10.36 suggests the market has high expectations for its future revenue growth. This is significantly higher than both the industry average of 3.92 and its competitor Sight Sciences's ratio of 2.39. I believe this high valuation multiple indicates that investors are willing to pay a premium for Glaukos' stock, likely due to the promising pipeline products like iDose TR and Epioxa. While this points to strong future growth potential, it's important also to note it exposes the stock to risk if these pipeline products face delays or do not achieve the expected market success. On the other hand, the much lower Price/Sales ratio for Sight Sciences might suggest it is undervalued or that the market perceives it to have less growth potential compared to Glaukos.

Conclusion

Based on the above factors affecting Glaukos, I think a HOLD rating is a prudent conclusion. While the company's impressive Q2 performance and robust product pipeline are signaling strong long-term growth potential, significant near-term risks warrant caution. Meanwhile, the high valuation, potential revenue disruption from the Photrexa to Epioxa transition, and intense competition from larger players like Regeneron and Roche create volatility. Therefore, I would suggest that waiting for clearer signals on regulatory approvals and market adoption is the most reasonable strategy at this time.

data.title: CRE: 2 Sectors Becoming Quite Concerning
data.description:
Today, I return to the deteriorating commercial real estate markets. While not nearly as large as the residential real estate market which is currently becoming toxic, it is an important part of the economy.

Outside of the Industrial property sector, delinquency rates on CRE loans have become concerning. While Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities or CMBS delinquency rates on both Retail and Lodging properties have moved up to around 6.5%, the two areas investors should have on their radar are the rapidly deteriorating Multifamily and Office sectors.

Let's start with Multifamily where CMBS delinquencies have more than doubled to 6.86% over the past 12 months. This delinquency rate does not capture loans that have been modified; it should be noted. Multifamily loans represent approximately $2.2 trillion of roughly $4.8 trillion of CRE debt outstanding.

The Multifamily space faces issues on multiple fronts right now. First, a good chunk of this debt was taken on during the ZIRP period when interest rates were much lower. Given commercial real estate loans typically run five to seven years, this debt load will have to be refinanced at significantly higher rates in the quarters and years ahead.

Some of this debt was funneled into the huge increase in apartments in the U.S. that have come online in recent years especially in areas that had benefited from the intrastate migration following Covid and its aftermath like Florida, Texas, and Tennessee. This resulted in 592,000 apartments opening in 2024. That was the best level since 1974, when the U.S. population was much smaller.

This obviously has contributed to a significant new rental supply becoming available. On the demand side, some 1.6 million net migrants have left the country in the first seven months of 2025. Notably, most are from self-deportation. With the significant increases for immigration enforcement that were incorporated into the recently passed 2026 federal budget, this is a trend that will likely continue in the coming quarters and years.

The combination of a large amount of supply coming on in recent quarters with falling demand on the margins is starting to show up in slowing rental growth (above). Importantly, this does not capture the increasing use of incentives (e.g., two months free on a 14-month lease) being utilized to address higher vacancies.

Unfortunately, the costs for property taxes and insurance are still increasing at a solid clip across much of the nation as are maintenance costs. You can see these pressures within the second quarter results from giant apartment REIT AvalonBay Communities, Inc. (AVB) where same-property revenue rose less than same-property expenses in the second quarter. It is little wonder the stock has moved down this year despite the rally in the overall market as well as the recent fall in interest rates.

Moving over to the developing disaster known as the Office sector. This accounts for approximately $1.3 trillion of the overall $4.8 trillion of CRE debt outstanding. CMBS delinquency rates have moved from under two percent in 2022 before the Federal Reserve embarked on the most aggressive monetary policy since the days of Paul Volcker, to nearly 11.7% in August. And again, this does not include loan modifications or other 'extend and pretend' measures taken by lenders.

Everything continues to work against this area of the commercial real estate sector. Office values have plunged in recent years in markets such as Los Angeles, Baltimore, and Chicago. Myriad buildings sport negative equity values with the collapse of values in many regions of the country. The national office vacancy rate reached 19.8% by the 2024 year-end. This was up 150bps on a year-over-year basis despite the lowest square footage of new office property coming online in 2024 since 2013.

The return to office push by major corporations like Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) and JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) stalled out overall in early 2023. There have been a few exceptions like NYC, which has seen office vacancy rates fall by solid margins. However, as I noted in an article earlier this week, the likelihood of Mamdani winning the mayoral election in November could present a new headwind on that front.

Nor can most of these properties be converted to other uses like residential real estate. The cost and structural changes needed to convert office buildings to condos only make financial sense for a small percentage of buildings.

Given these factors, I expect delinquency rates to continue to climb in this sector. Eventually, extend and pretend measures will end, and lenders will increasingly start to bite the bullet. This will be especially true if the country enters a recession. This will result in an uptick in foreclosures and higher credit losses for lenders.

So, who owns this debt? Commercial banks and thrifts own just less than 40%, Agency and GSEs like Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac hold just over 20% of these obligations, and life insurance companies hold just over 15%. Of note, Fannie Mae added just under $950 million to their credit loss provisions in their most recent quarter. This could complicate the potential efforts to bring it and Freddie Mac public again after government ownership since 2008.

In summary, these two parts of the commercial real estate market continue to deteriorate. While the approximate $3.5 trillion of debt obligations pales against the approximate $13 trillion in residential mortgage debt outstanding, it is still a considerable amount. More importantly, it is hard to see how this will not be increasing headwind to the U.S. economy in the quarters ahead.

data.title: Madison Square Garden Sports: Knicks And Rangers On Wall Street
data.description:
MSGS: two franchises, many constraints

If you follow the NBA or the NHL, you will certainly recognize the New York Knicks and the New York Rangers. What you may not know is that both franchises operate under the same company: Madison Square Garden Sports (NYSE:MSGS).

It is important to note that MSGS does not own the iconic arena itself. Instead, it operates under a long-term license with MSG Entertainment to use Madison Square Garden, and under a contract with MSG Networks for the local distribution of games played in New York. That means the company’s revenue mix is ultimately shaped by matchday income, sponsorship agreements, and media rights.

The problem, in my view, is that not everything depends on management execution. On the media side, for instance, the NBA has just signed a record-breaking national deal that will increase guaranteed payouts to teams starting in 2026. However, MSG Networks has had to restructure its debt and renegotiate payments lower, which cut the fee received by the Knicks and Rangers by 23% in a contract that runs until 2029.

Then there is the sporting and financial side, which I find particularly hard to project when margins depend on what happens either on the court or in the league offices. The NBA sets a salary cap for teams, and a luxury tax for those who exceed it. This year, the Knicks went over and will have to pay more than $29M as a result. On top of that, another $30M comes from revenue sharing. That is $60M hat will be redistributed to smaller franchises in order to keep the league balanced.

So, even with the Garden packed and global sponsorship contracts being signed, a record season like the last one can still end up with only marginal operating profit. That is why the most reasonable way I see to value MSGS is either through EV/Sales, which reflects how similar transactions have been priced, or through a sum-of-the-parts approach, since two franchises of this caliber may hold hidden value that the market has yet to fully recognize.

Knicks and Rangers set records, the income statement does not

The Dolan family has controlled MSGS since around 2010, and that grip was reinforced in 2020 after the separation of MSG Entertainment. Thanks to the Class B shares, they hold 70% of the voting power with less than 5% of the economic capital. In practice, that means nearly two decades of taking the strategic decisions that shape both the Knicks and the Rangers.

In the latest report, the company posted a historic revenue record above $1B, so it is worth breaking down the mix to see which verticals are contributing each dollar and to what extent.

The largest share comes from matchday, which accounts for almost a third of total revenues. Here we find ticket sales, season passes, and hospitality. With the Garden running at nearly 100% average occupancy and Knicks season tickets renewing at over 90%, there is enough evidence to confirm the strong momentum of the show.

The sponsorship vertical is also in good shape, especially after management announced during the last call the signing of a global contract that will take this line to record levels as well.

Closing out the mix is media, which in theory should be the most stable, yet has become the source of uncertainty. From this season onward, the NBA has signed an eleven-year deal with Disney, ESPN, NBC, and Amazon to distribute roughly $7B annually among the franchises. This is a notable jump compared with the $24B spread over nine years under the prior agreement.

The “but” comes from whether the renegotiation with MSG Networks can really offset the NBA deal. Previously, the Knicks and Rangers received close to $180M in local rights, but after the restructuring that figure has dropped by 23% to $138M. The effect was clear in Q3 FY25, revenues were down by $18.6M year-on-year, and annualized that translates into a hit of roughly $40M.

The paradox is evident. Knicks posting record revenues, sponsorships and renewals, Rangers filling the Garden, and yet operating profit ends up at just $38M after bringing in $1B.

With these numbers, I can only conclude that what happens on the court will be what sets MSGS’s path for the next year. The media side is clearly dragging results down, leaving everything in the hands of whether the Knicks and Rangers manage another deep playoff run.

And without further catalysts in sight, it becomes very hard to project a valuation on the assumption that the Knicks can go all the way this year.

From Madison to Wall Street: scenarios for MSGS

With the cut in MSG Networks, the luxury tax for exceeding the salary cap, and revenue sharing as one of the highest–earning franchises, margins are practically wiped out. That is why my valuation hypothesis is based either on EV/Sales or on a SOTP approach.

In terms of SOTP, according to Sportico the New York Knicks are valued at around $8B, and the Rangers at about $3.5B. Since this is not a private transaction we still have to apply some adjustments. From this $11B figure we need to subtract the net debt of about $1B.

And finally, I apply a 20% discount, because it is not the same to buy 100% of the business as it is to hold a minority stake, with no control. With these calculations, the net asset value would be around $7.8B, and dividing it between roughly 23M shares gives us a range of $330 to $340 per share.

The problem is that this NAV is just that, theoretical. Today MSGS trades at 5.7x EV/Sales FWD and at more than 50x P/FCF, multiples that look too demanding for an operating profit of only $38M. In private transactions we have seen sales around 12x EBITDA, such as the Suns in 2023, or around 10x EBITDA in the case of the Hornets.

In the NHL, Senators and Penguins have also traded in that 10–12x band. If we compare with football clubs like Manchester United (MANU) or Juventus (OTCPK:JVTSF), they trade at 3–4x EV/Sales, but their businesses are structurally weaker. For MSGS, EV/Sales seems reasonable, but a P/FCF of 20x would be the limit of what makes sense.

Breaking this down into scenarios makes it easier to understand. In a base year, with the Knicks reaching the second round and the Rangers making the playoffs, normalized FCF would be around $100M, which anchors the share price at $230. In a bear case, without a Knicks deep run and with local media revenues down, FCF would fall to $80–85M, justifying a range of $190–200. And in an optimistic case, with the Knicks in the Conference Finals and the Rangers alongside them, FCF would rise to $130–140M, which points to $250–260.

  • Deep run: revenues 1.1B, FCF 130–140M, multiple 6x EV/Sales FWD = $250 per share
  • Standard season: revenues 1.05B, FCF 90–100M, multiple 5.5x EV/Sales FWD = $230 per share
  • No playoffs: revenues 1B, FCF 80–85M, multiple 5x EV/Sales FWD = $180 per share

My base case is at least a standard season, and that anchors the share price at $230. There is not an excessive upside at these levels, and too many conditions to meet, but I lean toward a buy with a minimum target of $230 per share.

The market already draws its range: $190–$230

I always like to check the technical side to contrast my fundamental hypotheses, and this time was no different. What I see is that the price has been trapped since 2020 in a lateral range between $140 and $200, even though in 2022 a promising uptrend seemed to start.

When I cross this picture with what the options market is telling me for November 2025, I keep seeing that same lateral range, with puts concentrating around $190 and calls accumulating around $230. This confirms the hypothesis I have already set out, that the $230 per share scenario is what the market is pricing in unless a catalyst appears, whether a share buyback, a minority sale, or another deep run in the playoffs like last season.

I therefore reaffirm my view that at current prices around $200, with an EV/Sales FWD of 5.7x and a P/FCF of 50x, the multiples are too demanding to push the stock sustainably beyond $230 without that extra catalyst.

The risks that keep the discount

Now then, what could happen to invalidate my $230 per share scenario? I start from the assumption that the Knicks and the Rangers will perform in a similar way to last season, that the new national contract signed by the NBA will cover part of the hole left by MSG Network, and that this will leave the company with a stable FCF of around $100M. But I also see some risks that could easily derail that base case.

The audiovisual side is the one that worries me the most, because local media rights have already fallen 23%, and until 2029 that pressure will remain unless an alternative model appears once the current agreement ends.

Then we have the cost structure imposed by the leagues, both NBA and NHL. The Knicks exceed the salary cap almost every year, and as one of the top earners, they end up paying luxury tax and revenue sharing that instead of going down, could even rise further, since this is something MSGS cannot fully control.

And finally, the sports factor. What happens on the court inevitably shapes the operating result, and if the teams do not advance far enough in the competitions, the impact will be immediate in matchday and sponsorship revenues.

On top of this, there do not seem to be clear catalysts to the upside, beyond a strong season on the sporting front.

Buy, but with too many conditions

I keep a Buy rating on MSGS.

My conclusion is that despite the structural problems around local media rights, and the league rules on luxury tax and revenue sharing, MSGS still owns scarce and valuable sports assets.

With a NAV for the Knicks and Rangers that would easily exceed $300 even after applying a reasonable discount, there is still margin at current levels.

Of course, this is not a decision you can take just by looking at the accounting snapshot, because a P/FCF of 50x would be an automatic sell in other companies. But an EV/Sales FWD of 5.7x is not unreasonable for MSGS.

On top of that, the new NBA contract and the fact that MSGS is reporting record revenues lead me to this conclusion. I expect revenue stability, even with all the uncertainty around the business.

It will remain conditioned by what happens on the court, and nothing guarantees that the teams will deliver the results needed this season to push the stock to $230. But on a 12-month view, I do see it as feasible if they can repeat a season similar to the last one.

data.title: Alliance Entertainment: Margins, Cash, And More Keep Me Bullish
data.description:
Thesis

I initiated coverage of Alliance Entertainment Holding Corporation (NASDAQ:AENT) as a Buy several months ago. This was probably going against the consensus for this microcap by arguing the market was "missing" a turnaround driven by better operations, automation, cleaner inventory, a growing set of exclusive licensing deals, and a very unique, let’s call it a very “old school” business model. Meanwhile, cash flow was improving, leverage was coming down, and the valuation implied very low expectations.

So far, so good. The market has backed up my thesis and AENT has doubled in price (since my buy call), while the S&P 500 (SP500) is up roughly 11%.

A quick and easy conclusion here would be that the call has played out and it's time to harvest. Maybe. In this update I’ll examine the latest earnings report, highlight what changed in the business and the balance sheet, and update my rating.

Alliance Entertainment: Margins Lead Despite Sales Dip

Overall, things are looking positive for Alliance’s product mix. Holiday shopping is expected to grow at a moderate pace, with online sales leading the way. The U.S. has also extended Section 301 tariff exclusions on some goods imported from China until late November. According to management, tariffs don’t really affect music and video products much, but they do impact collectibles. Even so, Alliance has been able to pass along the higher costs to customers without hurting demand.

In this latest Q4, Alliance brought in $227.8 million in revenue, which was down 3.9% from the same time last year. Even though sales dipped, the types of products sold (the mix) and how well the company executed still made a big difference. Gross profit jumped 34% to $36.0 million. On top of that, the gross margin improved to 15.8%, up from 11.4% a year earlier. This improvement came from selling more higher-margin products and from efficiency gains thanks to automation.

Profitability improved as well. Net income came in at $5.8 million, which works out to $0.11 per diluted share, more than double the $0.05 per share earned a year ago. Adjusted EBITDA also climbed sharply to $12.2 million, up from just $2.1 million last year.

The company also became more efficient. Distribution and fulfillment costs dropped by about 1 percentage point as a share of revenue, thanks to its automation hub in Kentucky and a smaller footprint in Minnesota.

The company earned a little less money in total sales this year compared to last. They brought in $1.06 billion, while the year before they had $1.10 billion. And even though those sales dipped slightly, Alliance actually kept more money from what it sold. That’s because its gross profit went up to $132.9 million. This made its gross margin rise from 11.7% to 12.5%.

That improvement in efficiency carried over into earnings. Net income came in at $15.1 million. On a per-share basis, that’s $0.30, which is more than 3x what it was the previous year at $0.09. The EBITDA figure jumped by 51%, reaching $36.5 million.

Balance Sheet Gains and Structural Leverage
